title: Kundalini and Forgiveness: Why Awakening Forces You to Let Go

body:
Kundalini and Forgiveness: Why Awakening Forces You to Let Go

Hey, I get it. You're probably reading this because you're in it. That swirling, terrifying, utterly confusing mess that they call "awakening." Or maybe you just feel like you're losing your mind, and you stumbled here looking for answers. Either way, you're not alone. I've been there. I've been in that dark, cold sweat, heart-pounding terror, wondering if I'd ever feel normal again. And let me tell you, forgiveness - real, gut-wrenching, soul-deep forgiveness - isn't some fluffy spiritual concept when you're going through this. It's survival. It's the only way out.

You see, when kundalini wakes up, it doesn't ask politely. It kicks down the door of your consciousness and starts ripping through everything you thought you knew. All those old wounds, those resentments you buried so deep you forgot they were there? Yeah, they're coming up. And they're not coming up gently. They're coming up like a volcanic eruption, spewing out all the toxic sludge you've been carrying around. You can't run from it, you can't hide from it, and you sure as hell can't pretend it's not happening. This isn't a walk in the park; it's a forced excavation of your soul. Right?

I remember nights staring at the ceiling, feeling like my body was vibrating apart, my mind replaying every single slight, every betrayal, every stupid mistake I'd ever made. It felt like I was being punished, like all my past transgressions were being paraded before me in excruciating detail. It's not punishment, though. It's purification. And the biggest, nastiest block to that purification? Unforgiveness. It's like trying to drain a swamp with a plug in the bottom. That plug is your refusal to let go of the past, to forgive others, and - this is the kicker - to forgive yourself. You can't move forward when you're chained to yesterday's pain. You just can't. Know what I mean?

The Unbearable Weight of Resentment

So, why does awakening force this issue so hard? Because resentment, anger, bitterness - they're heavy. They're dense energy. And kundalini is all about raising your vibration, clearing out the old, making space for the new. It's like a high-powered vacuum cleaner for your soul. It's going to suck up all that stagnant emotional gunk, whether you like it or not. And if you're holding onto grudges, it's going to hit those spots like a freight train. It'll show you exactly how much those feelings are weighing you down, how much they're constricting your energy flow. It's not subtle. It's never subtle.

I used to think I'd forgiven people. Oh, I'd say the words. "Yeah, I've moved past that." But deep down, there were still those little hooks, those tiny barbs of anger and hurt, still lodged in my heart. When kundalini hit, those hooks became massive, painful anchors. Every time I thought of someone who'd wronged me, or something I'd done that I regretted, it was like a jolt of raw electricity. My body would tense up, my heart would race, and I'd feel this intense pressure. It wasn't just mental anymore; it was physical, visceral. That's the energy trying to move, trying to release, and hitting a wall of your own making.

You can try to suppress it, but during an awakening, that's like trying to hold back a tsunami with a teacup. It's futile, and it's going to make things infinitely worse. The energy has to move. It has to clear. And if you're resisting that clearing by clinging to old wounds, you're just creating more internal friction, more pain, more chaos. That's why so many people going through this feel like they're losing their minds - they're fighting an internal battle against the very force that's trying to heal them. It's heartbreaking to watch, and it's even harder to live through. But here's the thing: you have a choice. You can resist and prolong the agony, or you can surrender to the process and let go. Sounds simple, right? It's not. But it's necessary.

The Price of Holding On

What does holding onto unforgiveness cost you during an awakening? Everything. It costs you peace. It costs you sleep. It costs you your sanity. It manifests as physical pain, anxiety, depression, rage, confusion. It can feel like your nervous system is on fire, because it is. Those unresolved emotions are literally burning through your system, and if they can't be released, they just fester and create more internal pressure. I've seen people get stuck for years, just because they couldn't or wouldn't forgive. They get caught in a loop of pain, constantly replaying old scenarios, constantly feeling the sting of past hurts. It's a prison of your own making, and kundalini will shine a spotlight on every single bar of that prison.

It's not about condoning what happened. It's not about saying "it was okay." Forgiveness isn't for the other person; it's for you. It's about severing the energetic cord that ties you to that pain, that person, that event. It's about taking back your power from the past. When you refuse to forgive, you're basically giving that person or situation free rent in your head, free reign over your emotional state. And when your energy is already going haywire from an awakening, you absolutely cannot afford to give away that much control. You need every ounce of your energy to integrate what's happening. Every. Ounce. Does that land?

I found journaling to be incredibly helpful during this time. Just getting all that swirling, toxic thought out of my head and onto paper was a relief. I used a Leuchtturm1917 Hardcover Dotted Notebook - A5 (paid link) because it felt substantial, like my messy thoughts deserved a good home. And sometimes, I'd just write endless pages of rage, tears, and accusations, and then I'd burn them. Literally. It felt symbolic, a way of releasing that energy. It's not magic, but it helps. Really helps.

The Mirror of Self-Forgiveness

Here's the kicker, the one that most people miss, and the one that nearly broke me: self-forgiveness. You can forgive everyone else under the sun, but if you're still holding yourself accountable for every mistake, every misstep, every perceived failure, you're still holding onto that dense, heavy energy. Kundalini will force you to look at every single thing you've ever done that you feel guilty or ashamed about. And it will not let you off the hook until you make peace with it.

I had to face so many decisions I regretted, words I'd said, actions I'd taken. The shame was suffocating. It felt like I was reliving all of it, sometimes in vivid flashbacks, sometimes as just a persistent, nagging ache in my soul. I'd beat myself up relentlessly. "How could you be so stupid?" "Why did you do that?" "You deserved what happened." Sound familiar? You're not alone. This is a common part of the process. The energy is trying to clear the self-judgment, the self-loathing, the internalized criticism that you've been carrying around for years. It's trying to show you that you're worthy of love, even with all your imperfections. Especially with all your imperfections.

Look, you're human. You've made mistakes. Everyone has. And during an awakening, those mistakes are put under a microscope. It's uncomfortable, it's painful, and it can feel utterly unbearable. But it's also an opportunity - a brutal, terrifying opportunity - to finally, truly accept yourself. To forgive yourself for not being perfect, for not knowing better at the time, for being exactly who you were. Because until you can forgive yourself, you're constantly fighting against your own being. And when your energy is already in overdrive, that internal war is just going to tear you apart. You can't integrate higher states of consciousness when you're at war with yourself. It's impossible.

I found that working with the concept of "Morning Pages" from The Artist's Way Morning Pages Journal by Julia Cameron (paid link) helped me immensely with this. Just three pages, first thing in the morning, uncensored. It's not about writing anything deep; it's about getting all the junk out of your head. The self-criticism, the worries, the anger, the fear. Just dump it all. It creates space. It's like cleaning out the mental attic so you can actually see what's in there. And once you see it, you can start to address it. You can start to forgive it.

The Release: What Forgiveness Feels Like

So, what happens when you actually manage to forgive? It's not a light switch. It's not a sudden "poof!" and all your problems disappear. It's a gradual release, a softening. Forgiveness is a process, not a destination, especially when you're in the throes of an awakening. But when it happens, even in small increments, you feel it. You feel the tension leave your body. You feel a little lighter, a little more spacious inside. The obsessive thoughts about that person or event start to fade. The emotional charge lessens. It's like untying a knot that's been in your gut for years.

I remember one specific moment. I'd been struggling with a deep resentment towards someone from my past. It was like a constant dull ache in my chest. I'd tried everything - talking about it, writing about it, even screaming about it. Nothing worked. One night, during a particularly intense bout of energy surges, I just collapsed into tears. And in that moment of complete surrender, I just said, "I forgive them. I really do. I forgive myself for holding onto this for so long." And it wasn't some grand, spiritual revelation. It was just a quiet, exhausted acceptance. And then, for the first time in years, that ache in my chest was gone. It wasn't completely gone forever - sometimes it would try to creep back - but the power it had over me was broken. That's what true forgiveness does. It breaks the chains.

You'll find that as you let go of unforgiveness, the energy starts to flow more smoothly. Those terrifying surges might still happen, but they'll feel less like an attack and more like a cleansing. Your body might still vibrate, but it'll feel less like you're coming apart and more like you're being recalibrated. The intensity doesn't necessarily go away, but your relationship to it changes. You're no longer fighting against it; you're allowing it to do its work. And its work is to clear out everything that's not serving your highest good, everything that's blocking your true self from emerging. And trust me, unforgiveness is a massive block.

For some people, the sheer intensity of the awakening is enough to force forgiveness. You get so exhausted by the constant internal battle that you just give up. You say, "Okay, I can't do this anymore. I surrender." And in that surrender, the forgiveness often naturally arises. It's not a weakness; it's a deep strength. It's choosing peace over prolonged suffering. And during an awakening, that choice is everything.

Tools for the Journey

Okay, so how do you actually do this? Because saying "just forgive" is easy, but actually feeling it is a whole different ballgame, especially when you're feeling like a raw nerve ending. Here are some things that helped me, and that I've seen help others.

  • Radical Acceptance: This is huge. Accept that what happened, happened. Accept that you feel what you feel. Don't judge it, don't try to push it away. Just acknowledge it. "I'm furious right now. I'm hurt. This is what's here." That acceptance alone starts to loosen the grip of the emotion.
  • Journaling, again: Seriously, write it all out. Don't censor. Don't worry about grammar or spelling. Just let it flow. Get all that toxic gunk out of your head and onto the page. Sometimes you'll surprise yourself with what comes out. It's like talking to a therapist, but it's free and always available.
  • Meditation and Visualization: This can be tough during an awakening because your mind is often so chaotic. But even short bursts can help. Visualize yourself cutting the cords of attachment to the person or event you need to forgive. Imagine a light filling your heart, dissolving the bitterness. It's not about forcing it, but gently inviting it.
  • Compassion for Self and Others: This is hard, but try to see the situation from a broader perspective. Can you find even a tiny bit of compassion for the person who hurt you? Maybe they were hurting too. Maybe they didn't know any better. And for yourself - can you look at your past mistakes with kindness instead of judgment? You were doing the best you could with what you had at the time.
  • Creative Expression: Sometimes, words aren't enough. Painting, drawing, coloring - anything that gets you out of your head and into your body can be incredibly cathartic. I found that simple things like a Mandala Coloring Book for Adults - Stress Relief (paid link) could provide a much-needed mental break and a way to process emotions non-verbally.
  • Professional Support: Don't try to go it alone. This is critical. If you're struggling, find someone who understands spiritual emergence. A therapist, a coach, a spiritual guide. Someone who can hold space for you and help you deal with this terrifying world. I wish I'd had more support when I was going through it. Books like Waking Up Is Hard to Do: Navigating Spiritual Emergence (paid link) can be a lifeline, but they're not a substitute for human connection and guidance.

The Unfolding of Grace

When you commit to forgiveness, truly commit, something shifts. It's like you're telling the universe, "Okay, I'm ready to let this go. I'm ready to heal." And the universe responds. You might find synchronicities appearing, unexpected insights, moments of deep peace. It's not easy, I won't lie. There will be days you feel like you've made no progress, days you want to just curl up and disappear. But keep going. Keep choosing to let go, even if it's just for five minutes. Those five minutes add up.

The truth is, kundalini forces you to confront everything that's holding you back from your true, expansive self. And unforgiveness, whether towards others or yourself, is one of the biggest chains. It keeps you small, it keeps you stuck, and it keeps you in pain. The awakening process is a brutal, beautiful stripping away of everything that isn't you. And when you finally forgive, you're not just letting go of a burden; you're stepping into a new version of yourself. A lighter, freer, more authentic version. It's terrifying, yes. But it's also the most liberating thing you'll ever do. So yeah, you've got this. One breath, one tear, one act of forgiveness at a time.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
